coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots co-catalyzed with multiwalled carbon nanotubes and silver nanoparticles modified nanosensor for the electrochemical assay of anti-HIV drug Rilpivirine
coredata.dc:creator: Aftab, Saima
Kurbanoglu, Sevinc
Ozcelikay, Goksu
Bakirhan, Nurgul K.
Shah, Afzal
Ozkan, Sibel A.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel and sensitive voltammetric nanosensor has been developed for the first time, for the detection of Rilpivirine based on amine-functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (NH2-fMWCNT) with Ag nanoparticles onto carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode. Scanning electron microscopy, cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were employed for characterization of the modified electrode. The Rilpivirine showed two irreversible oxidation peaks at 1.20 V and 1.42 V, at all the investigated pH values. The cyclic voltammetry results demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrode toward the oxidation of Rilpivirine as endorsed by the enhanced current responses compared to bare electrode. The electrochemical catalytic activity was further utilized as a sensitive detection method for the investigation of the redox mechanism of Rilpivirine using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). For experimental conditions optimization the influence of supporting electrolyte and pH was examined and 0.5 M H2SO4 was selected as the best electrolyte for getting intense current signals of the target analyte. The relationship of anodic peaks potentials for peak 1 and peak 2 (EP1 and EP2) with pH values and scan rate was also studied. Scan rate results showed that the oxidation of Rilpivirine at the nanosensor surface occurs under adsorption controlled manner. Therefore, differential pulse adsorptive stripping voltammetric technique was employed for the determination of Rilpivirine. Optimum accumulation potential and time were found as 0 V and 60 s, respectively. Under these optimum conditions, response of Rilpivirine demonstrated a linear behavior in the concentration range from 1.00 × 10−9 to 7.00 × 10-9 M, with a limit of detection value of 3.00 × 10-11 M and 6.40 × 10-11 M for peak 1 and peak 2 in aqueous medium containing 0.5 M H2SO4 as supporting electrolyte, respectively. Interferences studies were achieved in the presence of 500 fold higher concentration of interfering agents to check the selectivity of the developed method. The designed method was successfully applied for the determination of Rilpivirine in biological fluids, urine and synthetic human serum as a real sample. The value of limit of detection were found to be 1.79 × 10-10 M, 4.47 × 10-10 M in serum samples, 5.26 × 10-10 M and 8.27 × 10-10 M in urine samples for peak 1 and peak 2, respectively. Recovery experiments were carried out to check the accuracy and precision of the designed method. Moreover, the repeatability, reproducibility and stability of the modified electrode in supporting electrolyte, serum and urine samples were investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rilpivirine
Nanosensor
Multiwalled carbon nanotubes
Carbon quantum dots
Silver nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of real samples of urine and serum
Electrochemical characterization of the modified electrode
Surface morphological characterization of CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE
Optimization of amount of CQDs, NH2-fMWCNT and AgNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Nyquist plots and (B) Cyclic voltammograms (100 mVs−1) recorded at; a) Bare GCE b) NH2 fMWCNT; c) CQDs/NH2 fMWCNT d) CQDs/NH2 fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE in 5 mM K4Fe (CN)6 +3 M KOH solution. Frequency range is from 0.1to 100 kHz for EIS.
(A) SEM images of Bare GCE; (B) NH2-fMWCNTmodified GCE; (C) NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE; (D) CQDs NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE; (E) EDX spectra of CQDs/ NH2 fMWCNT/AgNPs.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of 1 × 10−5 M RIL at; a) Bare GCE b) NH2-fMWCNT/CQDs/AgNPs modified GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution; (B) DPV of 40 μM RIL at (a) Bare electrode (b) COOHfMWCNT/CQDs modified electrode (c) NH2-fMWCNT modified electrode (d) COOHfMWCNT modified electrode (e) NH2 fMWCNT/CQDs modified electrode (f) NH2-fMWCNT/CQDs/AgNPs modified GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

DPV of (a) CQDs/NH2 fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 (without drug), DPV of 50 μM RIL at (b) 10:10:10 ratio of CQDs/NH2 fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE (c) 7:7:7 ratio of CQDs/NH2 fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE (d) 3:3:3 ratio of NH2 fMWCNT/CQDs/AgNPs modified GCE (e) 5:5:5 ratio of NH2 fMWCNT/CQDs/AgNPs modified GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.
(A) Anodic DPVs of 25 μM RIL solution using CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs/GCE in different pH (0.1–6.5); (B) Plots of Ep1 and I p1 vs. pH and Plots of Epa2 and I pa2 vs. pH.
Cyclic voltammograms of 1 × 10−5 M RIL in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution as supporting electrolyte using CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GC electrode at various scan rates (5- 100 mVs-1).
Effect of accumulation potential (A) & (B) and accumulation time (C) & (D) on peak currents of 10 μM RIL using CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs/GCE in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution.

(A) AdSDPV obtained at the CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs/GCE for RIL (in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution) at different concentrations; inset (B) is the calibration plot for RIL, (C) relationship of peaks current response to different concentration of RIL in the Human serum and (E) in urine; insert (D & F) are the calibration curves in serum and urine samples, respectively under optimum condition (i.e. tac = 60 s, Eac = 0 V and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution as supporting electrolyte).

(A) AdSDPV current response of 20 nM RIL at CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified electrode using 0.5 M H2SO4 as supporting electrolyte in the presence of interfering agents; (B) Effect of (A) pure RIL (B) ascorbic acid, (C) uric acid, (D) Mg2+, (E) Fe2+, (F) dopamine (G) glucose on the peak currents of RIL under optimum conditions. Black is I p1 and red is I p2, for pure RIL, pink is I p1 and green is I p2 in the presence of interfering agents. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Parameters calculated from the Nyquist plots and cyclic voltammograms.
Regression data of the calibration curves for the determination of RIL.

row.entry: Stability of modified GCE after 4 weeks (RSD %) a
Reproducibility of electrodes between days (RSD %) a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each value is average of three measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery results obtained from the determination of RIL in biological fluids.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each value is the main of five experiment.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots co-catalyzed with multiwalled carbon nanotubes and silver nanoparticles modified nanosensor for the electrochemical assay of anti-HIV drug Rilpivirine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ankara University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, 06560, Ankara, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Quaid-i-Azam University, Islamabad, 45320, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hitit University, Faculty of Art & Science, Department of Chemistry, Corum, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Bahrain, Sakhir, 32038, Bahrain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel and sensitive voltammetric nanosensor has been developed for the first time, for the detection of Rilpivirine based on amine-functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes (NH2-fMWCNT) with Ag nanoparticles onto carbon quantum dots modified glassy carbon electrode. Scanning electron microscopy, cyclic voltammetry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were employed for characterization of the modified electrode. The Rilpivirine showed two irreversible oxidation peaks at 1.20 V and 1.42 V, at all the investigated pH values. The cyclic voltammetry results demonstrated excellent electrocatalytic activity of the modified electrode toward the oxidation of Rilpivirine as endorsed by the enhanced current responses compared to bare electrode. The electrochemical catalytic activity was further utilized as a sensitive detection method for the investigation of the redox mechanism of Rilpivirine using differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). For experimental conditions optimization the influence of supporting electrolyte and pH was examined and 0.5 M H2SO4 was selected as the best electrolyte for getting intense current signals of the target analyte. The relationship of anodic peaks potentials for peak 1 and peak 2 (EP1 and EP2) with pH values and scan rate was also studied. Scan rate results showed that the oxidation of Rilpivirine at the nanosensor surface occurs under adsorption controlled manner. Therefore, differential pulse adsorptive stripping voltammetric technique was employed for the determination of Rilpivirine. Optimum accumulation potential and time were found as 0 V and 60 s, respectively. Under these optimum conditions, response of Rilpivirine demonstrated a linear behavior in the concentration range from 1.00 × 10−9 to 7.00 × 10-9 M, with a limit of detection value of 3.00 × 10-11 M and 6.40 × 10-11 M for peak 1 and peak 2 in aqueous medium containing 0.5 M H2SO4 as supporting electrolyte, respectively. Interferences studies were achieved in the presence of 500 fold higher concentration of interfering agents to check the selectivity of the developed method. The designed method was successfully applied for the determination of Rilpivirine in biological fluids, urine and synthetic human serum as a real sample. The value of limit of detection were found to be 1.79 × 10-10 M, 4.47 × 10-10 M in serum samples, 5.26 × 10-10 M and 8.27 × 10-10 M in urine samples for peak 1 and peak 2, respectively. Recovery experiments were carried out to check the accuracy and precision of the designed method. Moreover, the repeatability, reproducibility and stability of the modified electrode in supporting electrolyte, serum and urine samples were investigated.

ce:section.ce:para: Human Immunodeficiency Virus (HIV) is a worldwide deadly dilemma [1]. Several pharmaceutical anti-HIV drugs have been used for controlling this issue of grave concern. Ideally, an anti-HIV medication should be very dynamic against HIV-1, have insignificant side impacts and permitting once-every day oral treatment at relatively low doses [2–4]. Among them, Rilpivirine (RIL), (TMC278{(4-[[4-[[4-[(1E)-2-cyanoethenyl]-2,6-dimethylphenyl]amino]-2-pyrimidinyl]amino]benzonitrile} [5,6], is a new diarylpyrimidine (DAPY) nonnucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor that was formulated in a multidisciplinary structure-based design exertion [7] planned to find drugs that could inhibit an extensive variety of the known drug-resistant HIV-1 strains. Phase I and II clinical preliminaries have demonstrated that TMC278 successfully diminishes viral load and keeps up low viral load levels with no infection bounce back in patients even following 48 weeks of treatment [8,9]. RIL is used either alone or in combination with other HIV drugs such as Tenofovir and Emtricitabine. RIL is used in the treatment of HIV infection at relatively low oral dose of 25–75 mg/day [10]. Rilpivirine is effective for HIV treatment at 25 mg daily oral dose. It is reported that, cord blood Rilpivirine plasma concentration can be between 10.0 and 101.5 ng.mL−1. It is reported that, 85% of drug is eliminated in feces (25% as unchanged rilpivirine) and 6.1% in urine (almost exclusively as metabolites) [10,11]. Overdose of RIL is associated with vomiting, skin rash, liver problem, depression, fever and swelling of body parts [12]. In order to inhibit the adverse effects associated with a drug, analysis is very critical for checking the drug sample. Underdose or overdose of a drug can cause serious diseases or may cause significant effects. Therefore, observing the performance of a drug plays key role in clinical applications. Several techniques have been used for the detection of drugs in biological fluids and pharmaceutical formulations such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13], chromatography, spectrophotometry [14], liquid chromatography coupled with mass spectrometry [15], capillary electrophoresis [16,17], and HPLC coupled with UV/ fluorescence detection [18–20]. However, these strategies have numerous misfortunes like tedious planning, talented individual to work, massive instruments and unaffordable costs. Researchers are keenly interested in building up a simple methodology for the detection of drugs. In this regard electrochemical strategy is the best option for drug detection as it does not require any pre-treatment, it is simple to carry out and can be accessible on location of investigation [21,22]. Electrochemical methods utilizing modified electrodes can be considered for detection of pharmaceutical drugs as a powerful alternative. Among the electrochemical methods, differential pulse voltammetry is an effective technique for electrochemical detection on account of its characteristic features such as simplicity, sensitivity, and selectivity [23,24]. In electrochemical nanosensors, electrode itself can act as a sensor and its sensing ability can be improved by modification of its surface by nanomaterials. The modification of electrode surface can facilitate electron transfer between the electrode and analyte, thereby increasing the sensing ability of the electrode [25–27]. MWCNTs based electrochemical sensors show low limit of detection and quick response because of signal improvement given by high effective surface area and fast electrode kinetics. MWCNTs are extremely appealing for detecting applications due to their high electrical and thermal conductivity, chemical stability and mechanical strength. Furthermore, their high surface-to-volume ratio is additionally a distinct advantage towards the advancement of electrochemical sensing platform for sensitive drug detection [28–30]. In this regard, MWCNT are the best options as electrode modifiers as these can decrease the over potential and improve sensitivity. MWCNTs with metal nanoparticles (NPs) are significantly attractive because of unique physical and chemical properties [31–34]. In fact a number of researchers have used metal oxide NPs/metal NPs decorated nanocomposites for improvement of electrochemical sensors [35–41]. Among the metal NPs, AgNPs have pulled in significant enthusiasm for modification of electrode because of their ability to increase the electrode conductivity, which enhances selectivity and sensitivity [42,43]. Another advantage of AgNPs is their ability of facilitating more effective electron transfer e.g. harboring more active site on their surface [44]. In addition to NPs, quantum dots based electrochemical assays have become more and more fascinating due to their small size, high sensitivity and comparatively lower cost. Their ability of direct electron transfer between electrode and analyte has also been discovered [45–48]. Therefore, in order to take full benefits of the three types of nanomaterials MWCNT, AgNPs and QDs, it is attractive to make QDs/MWCNT/AgNPs nanocomposite, so that the individual properties of each material could be combined for inducing novel properties. Literature survey reveals that the electrochemical detection of RIL at a bare or modified electrode is an unexplored matter. Therefore, in the present study, amine group functionalized MWCNT, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and silver nanoparticles were used to fabricate a novel CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs nanocomposite modified glassy carbon electrode for the selective and sensitive determination of RIL. The repeatability, reproducibility and stability of the designed sensor were estimated and then it was successfully applied for the detection of RIL in biological samples.

RIL was kindly supplied by Johnson and Johnson (Istanbul, Turkey). Acetic acid, sodium acetate, phosphoric acid (>85%), potassium phosphate, methanol (99.8%), sodium hydroxide, sulphuric acid (99.99%), acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (≥99%), and carbon quantum dots (≥0.2% in water, λem =450–550 nm and quantum efficiency >5%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. NH2 group and COOH group functionalized multi walled carbon nanotubes (NH2-fMWCNT, COOH-fMWCNT) were supplied from DropSens. AgNPs (10 nm, containing 0.1 mM Ag in 20 mL water) was purchased from NanoCS®. N,N dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Merck. The drug free human serum was supplied from Sigma-Aldrich and urine sample was collected from healthy volunteer. Ultrapure water was used for the preparation of all solutions.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and voltammetric experiments i.e. cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and adsorptive stripping differential pulse voltammetry (AdSDPV) were performed by using AUTOLAB-PGSTAT 100 N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) electrochemical analyzer running with software NOVA 2.11. All the electrochemical experiments were performed in a conventional three electrodes system consisting of a working electrode, a reference electrode and a counter electrode. The bare glassy carbon electrode (GCE) with a diameter of 3.0 mm or modified GCE was used as working electrode for voltammetric analysis of RIL. Ag/AgCl immersed in saturated KCl solution (3 M) and a platinum wire were used as a reference and counter electrodes, respectively. All pH measurements were made using a pH meter Model 538 (WTW, Austria) with a combined electrode (glass electrode-reference electrode). The DP voltammograms were conducted with the following parameters: modulation amplitude: 0.025 V; step potential: 0.005 V; interval time 0.5 s.; modulation time: 0.05. For AdSDPV studies, optimum stripping conditions were obtained as follows: accumulation time: 60 s and accumulation potential: 0 V. ZEISS EVO 40 (Merlin, Carl Zeiss) was used to obtain scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images and EDX spectra. EV018 vacuum oven was used for drying the electrode. All the electrochemical measurements were operated under ambient conditions.

Prior to modification, the GCE was polished with 0.05 μm alumina slurry on a rubbing pad followed by thorough rinsing with ultrapure water and ethanol in ultrasonic bath. Then, an aliquot of 5 μL of CQDs solution was coated on the surface of GCE and vacuum dried for 10 min to obtain CQDs/GCE. After that 5 μL of NH2-fMWCNT (1 mg.mL−1) was applied on the CQDs/GCE by drop casting method and dried. The CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs/GCE was fabricated by casting 5 μL of AgNPs solution on the surface of CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/GCE and dried in vacuum. Further studies were then performed with the as prepared CQDs /NH2-fMWCNT/GCE.

Stock solution of 1 mM RIL was prepared by dissolving the required amount in DMSO. Required volume of the stock solution was transferred to the electrochemical cell containing 8 mL of supporting electrolyte and the total volume was made to 10 mL using DMSO to avoid the solubility problem. The 1 mg.mL−1 concentration of fMWCNT was prepared in DMF with the aid of ultrasonication for 2 h, resulting in the formation of a uniform black suspension. For interference studies, the required amounts of ascorbic acid, uric acid, dopamine, glucose and some inorganic compounds were dissolved in water. All stock solutions were diluted with supporting electrolyte to the desired concentration.

For analysis of the drug tablets, five tablets of Edurant® (each tablet containing 25.0 mg RIL) were weighted accurately and powdered in a mortar. A portion of this fine powder containing 84.03 mg of RIL was dissolved in 50.0 mL of DMSO in order to make 1.0 mM stock solution. It was then sonicated for 20 min and filtered. Appropriate solutions were prepared by taking suitable aliquots of this solution and diluting with the selected supporting electrolyte. To know whether the excipients present in pharmaceutical formulations show any interference, recovery experiments were performed and for this purpose, a known amount of the pure RIL was added to the dosage form of RIL. The resulting solutions were analyzed by AdSDPV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of real samples of urine and serum
ce:section.ce:para: For the preparation of serum standard solution, 1 mL of RIL from stock solution was transferred into a 10 mL centrifuge tube and then the solution was diluted with 3.6 mL of serum and 5.4 mL acetonitrile to make total volume of 10 mL. The mixture was centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 30 min to separate the protein residues and then the supernatant was taken carefully. Here, acetonitrile acted as a precipitating agent for protein residues. Then the investigation was followed up as shown in the basic analytical strategy. The calibration plot for serum samples was also prepared utilizing supporting electrolyte, which was helpful in recovery experiment. Urine standard solution was prepared in the same way as followed for preparation of serum solution. Then the solution was diluted with supporting electrolyte for calibration curve and recovery experiments. To check the accuracy of the designed sensor, recovery experiments were also performed in serum and urine samples, using standard addition method. All the measurements were achieved at least in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) can give information about the impedance change of the electrode interface and the semicircle diameter at the higher frequency region associate to the electron transfer resistance (Rct), which controls the electron transfer kinetics of the redox probe. EIS measurements were performed at both the unmodified and the modified GCE using 5 mM K4[Fe(CN)6] and 3 M KOH solution. The results are shown in Fig. 1
(A) in the form of Nyquist plot, demonstrating a semicircle in the high frequency region along with a straight line in the low frequency region. The Nyquist plots at the modified electrodes represent a relatively smaller semicircle as compared to the bare GCE suggesting faster electron transfer at the surface of modified electrodes. For analyzing impedance data, an equivalent circuit (inserted in Fig. 1 (A)) was used. Parameters obtained after fitting the data are listed in Table 1 , where Re stands for resistance of electrolytic solution and CPE for constant phase element. The charge transfer resistance of the modified electrodes decreased after the bare GCE was modified with CQDs, NH2-fMWCNT and AgNPs due to high surface to volume ratio and higher electrocatalytic behavior of CQDs, NH2-fMWCNT and AgNPs. With the formation of CQDs, NH2-fMWCNT and AgNPs nanocomposite, the diameter of semicircle of CQDs/ NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE was reduced compared to the other individually modified electrodes. The reason may be the synergistic effect of these materials that could cause improved electron transfer kinetics at the interface between electrode and solution. Moreover, the decrease in Rct values can be related to the electroactive surface of the electrode. To ensure this, electro-active surface areas of the bare electrode and modified electrodes were evaluated from cyclic voltammograms shown in Fig. 1 (B). The IP values at various scan rates are were plotted against ѵ 1/2 according to the Randles Sevcik equation, and active surface area was calculated from the slope values,
(3.1) IP = 2.69 × 105 n3/2AD1/2ѵ1/2C

In this equation A represents active surface area (cm2), IP the peak current (μA), n the number of electron transferred, D the diffusion coefficient and C the concentration of the redox probe. For the potassium ferrocyanide redox probe n is 1, D is 7.6 × 10−6 cm2s-1 at standard temperature and pressure and C is 5 mM. Table 1 enlists active surface area of each modified electrode. An increase in surface area was observed after modification. It can be seen that CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified electrode has greater active surface area as compared to other solely modified electrodes, which means CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE provides greater surface area to catalyze the respective reaction on its surface and facilitates the reaction more efficiently. The results obtained from cyclic voltammetry are in good agreement with the EIS. The decrease in Rct and increase in active surface area offer evidence about the successful modification of the electrode and it points out that CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs nanocomposite modified GCE is a suitable platform for electro sensing of the analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphological characterization of CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: In order to modify the surface of GCE, CQDs/ NH2-fMWCNT /AgNPs were anchored via layer-by-layer approach. Surface morphology of the CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE was determined by SEM armed with EDX analysis. SEM images provided an exmination of morphological features of modified GCE. The surface morphologies of (A) bare GCE (B) NH2-fMWCNT, (C) NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs and (D) CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs portrayed by SEM images are shown in Fig. 2 . The surface of bare GCE looks like a smooth and dark surface, while NH2-fMWCNT looks as randomly distributed tubes that completely cover surafce area of GCE. (C) represents well deposited AgNPs on the surface of NH2-fMWCNT. The CQDs and NH2-fMWCNT providing greater porosity may considerably increase the electroactive surface area of CQDs/NH2- fMWCNT/GCE, so these are expected to accumulate higher amount of AgNPs on their surface. AgNPs were deposited on CQDs/ NH2 fMWCNT/GCE to form a third layer. AgNPs further increased the electroactive surface area of the CQDs/NH2 fMWCNT/AgNPs /GCE (see Table 1). The SEM of CQDs/NH2- fMWCNT/AgNPs/GCE shows up as NH2-fMWCNT embed the CQDs and AgNPs in its structure. Such interconnected CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs system may build up electrical conduction pathway throughout the entire nanocomposite, which is responsible for the electrical conductivity and electrochemical sensing. Fig. 2 E shows the EDX spectra, which confirms the presence of AgNPs, NH2-fMWCNT, and CQDs on the electrode surface.

Literature survey revealed that no previous electrochemical studies are available neither on bare electrode nor on a modified electrode about the sensitive electroanalytical determination of RIL in biological fluids. Cyclic voltammetry was used to investigate the electrochemical behavior of RIL on the CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs nanocomposite modified and bare GCE in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution as a supporting electrolyte. At the bare GCE (Fig. 3
a), RIL shows an irreversible behavior with two oxidation peaks at EP1 =1.238 V and EP2 =1.423 V with anodic peak current of IP1 = 2.320 μA and IP2 = 1.170 μA, respectively. However, at CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified electrode (Fig. 3 b) the oxidation peaks currents of RIL are significantly increased with IP1 = 65.49 μA and IP2 = 36.743 μA, respectively. Thus, the enhancement in peak currents along with the decrease in the peak potentials at the modified electrode signify that the CQDs, NH2-fMWCNT and AgNPs exhibit efficient electrocatalysis towards oxidation of RIL. In addition to the electrocatalytic activity of these nanocomposites, the better response of CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE in comparison to NH2-fMWCNT/GCE, CGQs/GCE and bare electrode can be attributed to the difference in electroactive surface areas. The CQDs increases the space for the attachment of more amount of NH2-fMWCNT, which further increases the electroactive surface area of the CQDs modified GCE due to sp2 hybridization of MWCNTs, resulting in the π-π interactions between the adjacent layers of MWCNTs forming more structural defects for attracting more AgNPs on the surface. Thus the formation of greater active surface area CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs electrochemical sensor improves the electrode kinetics and enhances the electrocatalytic effect of RIL to attain IP1 = 65.49 μA and IP2 = 36.743 μA as already confirmed by EIS and CV results. Among the pulse techniques, DPV was used in order to verify the CV results as it has several advantages including its high sensitivity, good resolution and lower background currents. It has become the analytical technique of potential interest for research as it allows analysis of multi-component analyte system with high sensitivity. Keeping these characteristic features in consideration, DPV was used for the investigation of RIL. DP voltammograms of 40 μM RIL were recorded with different modified electrodes and bare GCE as shown in Fig. 3 (B). Similar to CV, two oxidation peaks were observed corresponding to the amine groups of the RIL. One can see that the COOH-fMWCNT modified electrode increases the intensity of only one peak; moreover, on combination with CQDs its electrocatalytic activity is declined. In contrast, the intensity of both peaks increases on modification of electrode with NH2-fMWCNT, CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT, and CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs. The CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified GCE shows the highest peak current among the modified electrodes. The first oxidation peak at the nanocomposite modified electrode is nearly 17 times higher than that at bare electrode. However, the most stunning results were obtained with CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs/GCE with maximum increase of current that could best be attributed to the synergistic effect of the components of CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs nanocomposite modified electrode. They trap RIL within their porous structure and provide more binding site, thus significantly contributing towards accumulation of more of RIL at the modified GCE surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of amount of CQDs, NH2-fMWCNT and AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: From CV, DPV and EIS results, it was confirmed that CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified electrode is suitable for developing into a highly sensing platform for the determination of RIL. All the techniques as layer-by-layer or mixing, or the sequence changing were tried, Hence, CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified electrode was used for further studies. After selection of modifying agents, the next very important step is optimization of the amounts of modifying agents on the electrode surface. The effect of amount of CDQs, NH2-fMWCNT and AgNPs as modifier on the surface of electrode was investigated by varying its composition (Fig. 4 ). Different ratios of modifying agents were tried by changing the ratio of one modifier while keeping fixed ratio of other two modifiers. It was then found that the oxidation current of both peaks increased with increasing amount of these three nanomaterials up-to ratio of 5:5:5 μL; beyond this ratio, a decrease in the intensity of peaks current was noticed. As a result, ratio of 5:5:5 μL was selected as the optimum coated amount for the preparation of CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs for the electrode surface.

In general, it is critical to select buffer solution and pH of the buffer solution in voltammetric analysis. The pH of the supporting electrolyte has a prominent effect on the electro-redox reactions of analytes in light of the fact that, protons are constantly engaged with the electrochemical responses and apply a critical impact on the peak potential and current. With this consideration, the impact of pH of supporting electrolyte on the oxidation peak currents and potentials of RIL was investigated in the pH range from 0.1 to 6.5 employing phosphate and acetate buffer and sulphuric acid solution. The RIL response disappeared at pH 7.0 and higher pH values. It was observed that with the gradual increase in pH of the supporting electrolyte, the peak potentials shifted towards less positive values, which demonstrates the involvement of protons during the oxidation process. At very low pH values, both anodic peaks are well resolved and clearly separated from each other. As the pH increased, the anodic peaks current decreased and above pH 5.7, both peaks became unsymmetrical and tended to merge with each other as shown in Fig. 5
(A). Over the pH range 0.1–6.5, the peak potentials Ep1 and Ep2 of RIL varied linearly as a function of pH see Fig. 5 (B). The values of slopes suggested that an unequal number of electrons and protons are involved in the oxidation reactions of RIL for both peaks, according to Nernst equation. The results showed that the largest peak current and the best peak shape for RIL was obtained in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution and thus this supporting electrolyte was chosen for further electroanalytical studies.

Ep - pH behavior followed the following linear regression equations:
(3.2) Ep1 = 1.062 - 0.0276 pH; (R2 = 0.998)
(3.3) Ep2 = 1.223 - 0.0262 pH; (R2 = 0.999)

From the electrochemical studies, valuable information, for example, oxidation/reduction mechanisms, nature of the reaction on the electrode surface either diffusion or adsorption controlled and kinetics parameters are normally can be attained from the peak current/potential and scan rate relationships. Therefore, influence of scan rates on the oxidation peak currents of RIL was evaluated by CV. Fig. 6 shows the CVs of 1 × 10−5 M RIL at various scan rates (5 − 100 mV s−1) using CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs/GCE. It is evident from the scan rate effect on the RIL system that intensity of peaks current (IP1 and IP2) increases with increase of scan rate and peaks potentials (EP1 and EP2) are shifted towards more positive values. The positive shift in the peaks potentials with the scan rate corresponds to irreversible nature of electron transfer processes. To confirm that the process is either controlled by adsorption or by diffusion, the relation between logarithms of peak currents were plotted versus logarithm of scan rate and straight lines were obtained (Fig. SI.1.) according to the following expressions:
(3.4) log I p1 (μA) = 0.997 log ν – 0.060 (R2 = 0.997)
(3.5) log I p2 (μA) = 1.092log ν – 0.624 (R2 = 0.999)

According to literature, the slope value of plot of log of peak current versus log of scan rate close to 0.5 represents diffusion-controlled electrode process, whereas close to 1 suggests an adsorption-controlled process. In equations 3.4 and 3.5, the value of slope is very close to the theoretical value of 1 for peak 1 and for peak 2 which clearly, shows that the processes are adsorption controlled. Moreover, peak current versus scan rate was plotted (Fig. SI.1.) and a linear relationship was achieved according to following equations:
(3.6)
I p1 (μA) = 0.646ѵ (Vs−1) + 0.300 (R2 = 0.999)
(3.7)
I p2 (μA) = 0.366ѵ (Vs−1) ­ 0.86 (R2 = 0.999)

These linear relationship between peak currents (Ip1 and Ip2) and ѵ also confirm that adsorption mechanism is involved in the oxidation of RIL on the surface of modified electrode. The adsorption characteristics of RIL oxidation is further substantiated by nonlinear dependence of peak current on the square root of scan rate which is depicted from the plots of Ip1 and Ip2 versus square root of scan rate (Fig. SI.1.). The low correlation coefficient values of 0.956 and 0.954 for peak 1 and peak 2, in the plot of current versus square root of scan rate as compared to R2 values in Ip1 and Ip2 versus scan rate plots suggest that the oxidation of RIL is controlled by adsorption.
(3.8)
I p1 (μA) = 7.808ѵ 1/2(Vs−1)1/2 -18.167 (R2 = 0.956)
(3.9)
I p2 (μA) = 4.418ѵ 1/2(Vs−1)1/2 -11.269 (R2 = 0.954)

The dependence of the EP1 and EP2 and log ѵ (Fig. 5 E) gave a linear relationship with regression equations of 3.10 and 3.11 according to Laviron equation for irreversible system.
(3.10) EP1 (V) = 0.0502logѵ (mVs−1) +1.255 (R2 = 0.999)
(3.11) EP2 (V) = 0.055logѵ (mVs−1) +1.433 (R2 = 0.999)

Given the fact that RIL could be adsorbed at the electrode surface, the impact of accumulation potential and time on the peak current responses of RIL were explored. Accumulation step is broadly utilized in electroanalytical science to accumulate analyte and enhance the sensitivity. The dependence of current of the anodic peaks of RIL on accumulation potential was probed by DPV over the range of 0-0.9 V as depicted in Fig. 7
A&B. The decrease in the intensity of peak currents (IP1, IP2) was observed with change of accumulation potential from 0 to 0.9 V. For accumulation time, the intensity of anodic peak currents (IP1, IP2) increased gradually as the accumulation time increased up-to 60 s. With time longer than 60 s, peak current decreased and almost unchanged which pointed to the complete coverage of electrode surface within 60 s (see Fig. 7 C&D). Therefore, 0 V was selected as optimum accumulation potential and 60 s as optimum accumulation time for both peaks.

The DPV was employed to investigate the relationship between the peak currents and concentration of RIL, in order to check the feasibility of the method for the quantitative determination of RIL. Under optimum conditions, when the concentration of RIL changed from 1 × 10−9 to 7 × 10−9 M, a linear relationship between the peak currents (IP1 & IP2) and concentrations of RIL was observed as shown in Fig. 8
A. The regression equations were IP1 (μA) = 124.08C + 1 × 10-7 (R2 = 0.998) and IP2 (μA) = 37.31C + 3 × 10-8 (R2 = 0.999) for peak 1 and peak 2, respectively. The slopes of the two calibration curves (inset B) were used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD) and the limit of quantification (LOQ) using the following equations; LOD = 3σ/m and LOQ = 10σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation of peak current values for the lowest concentration of RIL and m is the slope of the calibration plot [51]. The LOD and LOQ were found to be 3.00 × 10-11 M and 9.08 × 10-11 M, 6.40 × 10-11 and 1.94 × 10-10 for peak 1 and peak 2, respectively in 0.5 M H2SO4. RIL was also determined in biological samples (Human serum and urine) (see Fig. 8 C & E) and linear calibration curves were obtained in the range of 3–15 nM (see inset D& F). The parameters calculated from the calibration curves are given in Table 2 .

To estimate the reproducibility of the proposed method of electrode fabrication, five different electrodes were fabricated independently and used for the determination of 2.5 μM RIL in the same day. Moreover, reproducibility of the nanosensor between days was also calculated and their relative standard deviation (RSD%) values are listed in Table 2. The RSD values less than 2% in supporting electrolyte and less than 5% in biological samples indicate good reproducibility of the proposed method. The intra-day and inter-day repeatability of the modified electrode were also investigated. To check repeatability, RSD% value of five successive measurements of peaks current of 2.5 μM at the modified electrode was calculated intra-day and inter-day (see Table 2). The stability of method was tested in the same concentration of RIL after four weeks. No significant change was observed in the current response of peaks after several DPV measurements. The RSD was found less than 2% thus suggesting excellent stability and repeatability of the designed nanosensor.

Some electroactive species in serum and the other auxiliary related medications may affect the performance of a sensor. So the selectivity of the designed method toward RIL was examined by introducing some biological interfering agents for example, glucose, ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and dopamine (DP). These compounds were individually introduced in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing 20 nM each to investigate the selectivity of the method. Fig. 9
A & B elucidate that current response of both peaks of RIL on modified electrode are not distinctly affected in the presence of 500-fold excess concentration of each of interfering agents. These findings reveal the ability of the designed sensor to maintain its robustness even in the presence of excess amount of interfering agents.

In order to evaluate the validity, applicability and accuracy of the designed sensor, the standard addition method was applied to assay RIL in Edurant® tablet dosage form, using the obtained calibration equation. The preparation of the pharmaceutical samples is given in experimental Section 2.4.1. An examination of Table 3 depicts that the content values measured by the designed method for the commercial dosage form are very close to the claimed amount.

To the check the reliability of the developed method for practical applications, recovery experiments were performed in pharmaceutical dosage form, human serum and urine samples by standard addition method using CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs modified electrode. The obtained results are listed in Table 3. The excellent recoveries in the range between 98.5% and 100.47% illustrate the effectiveness and reliability of the developed method for pharmaceutical dosage form and real sample analysis. Obviously, these results indicate that the proposed method is not affected from any matrix interferences coming from the excipients present in dosage form, endogenous substances from serum and urine samples, due to electrochemical inertness of these excipients and/or inactive ingredients. Therefore, the RIL amount can be quantitatively determined by the designed sensor, thus guaranteeing the accuracy of the voltammetric measurement of the RIL in routine pharmaceutical analysis and real sample.

In the present work, a new CQDs/NH2-fMWCNT/AgNPs nanocomposite modified nanosensor on GCE surface was successfully fabricated and then applied as a novel electrochemical sensor for the investigation of the electrochemical response of RIL in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. The current enhancing performance of the modified electrode was verified from the improved interfacial and surface area properties obtained from EIS and CV experiments. The ultra-sensitive detection of anti-HIV drug RIL was made for the first time by using electrochemical nanosensor. Effect of essential parameters such as amount of modifying agents, pH, and scan rate was studied to get the best response for the determination of RIL. The modified electrolyte dramatically boosted the current signals of the RIL compared to bare GCE. The stunning electrochemical performance of modified electrode may be explained by high surface area and high conductive nature of carbonaceous materials and AgNPs. The values of LOD and LOQ demonstrated excellent performance of the designed method for ultra-trace level detection of RIL. The developed sensor was found to maintain its selectivity in the presence of 500 fold higher concentration of foreign agents than the RIL. The developed method was applied to detect RIL in biological samples with LOD of 1.79 × 10−10 M, 4.47 × 10-10 M in serum sample and 5.26 × 10−10 M, 8.23 × 10−10 M, in urine sample, for peak 1 and peak 2, respectively. Hence, it can be concluded that our proposed nanosensor can easily detect and determine RIL in reported urine and serum levels. The developed nanosensor showed good repeatability, stability and reproducibility. Furthermore, the high recoveries from 98.5% to 99.9% demonstrated successful application of the developed method for real sample analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We kindly acknowledge to TUBITAK for BIDEB Program 2216. The authors thanks to Mohammad Salim Akhter for language editing. In addition, we kindly acknowledge and thanks to Johnson and Johnson (Istanbul, Turkey) for providing Rilpivirine standard compound and its pharmaceutical dosage form.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.094.
